content:
I grew up in Texas….
So I’m not exactly a stranger, to heat. 🥵




But 100+ Texas heat, WITH AC….
& 100+ Paris heat, WITHOUT AC….


…. are SO not the same thing. 😩





I’m being a baby—
I’ll be fine. 🥵🥺





But just wanted to say to everyone that called & checked in on me, that:


1️⃣  I’m alive.
2️⃣  Oui…. I’m still wildly in love with France, & no (Mom) I didn’t buy a Southwest plane ticket back to the US. 



3️⃣  don’t take ice cube makers, for granted.
4️⃣  who knew….. that shampoo can melt??

5️⃣y’all were super sweet to check on me.🙂


And 6️⃣,, I was supposed to post like 3 things this week….

But basically my keys, have melted into my computer. 






So I’d normally say—> you’ll have to wait until next week, for my “hot takes”….



But considering 3 of my candles legitimately melted over, without being lit….

it’s just too soon for the jokes. 🥵🙂





Bon weekend à tous!!
Et à la semaine prochaine. :)

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
…….ok y’all: here’s 5 cold email tips, to close out la semaine. :)



1️⃣  More pattern interruptive punctuation (& a few acronyms), the better.
2️⃣  5 emails to a “sequence”, over 21 business days.
3️⃣  Add a piece of written content (article, blog etc.) in email #2. & a piece of audio/video content (webinar, podcast etc.) in email #5.
4️⃣  Make sure the content is 3rd party. (Not written by you) & that focuses on solving a problem, for their specific buyer persona.
5️⃣   Pattern interrupt in the subject line. 
AKA look at the subject line & ask yourself, if you were the recipient….
 On a scale of 1 to 10, how much would YOU be able to tell that it’s a “sales email”.
 If it’s over an 8, then I’d re-write it. 

 An evergreen pattern-interruptive subject line, is to include a couple words of TRUE personalization.    EX. …. you said “endless options”, Ben.  



The key ISN’T that you spend hours of time on the personalization— OR pick irrelevant personalization.   But the key (or one of them), is that you’re SPECIFIC.  
 That you’re saying something that they would KNOW from looking at it, that it was written to just them.      One of the most common mistakes, is confusing being “pattern interruptive”, with being silly. 
And cracking a bunch of jokes.  

And the pattern they’re trying to interrupt is “being too stuffy”, or talking corporate.  

 The pattern that I would target, ISN’T “being too serious”……. 
The pattern that I would target, is that the norm, statistically, is that the seller doesn’t know anything about the buyer.  


 It’s a mass blast. Trying to cast a WIDE net.   Without actually knowing anything about the prospect, they’re sending the email too.   That’s what’s normal. That’s the “pattern”. 

  So to interrupt it, you say 2-3 words in the subject line…. That are written & truly personalized, to just them.

And proves to them in real time that you’ve invested in them, by doing your research.


It doesn’t earn you the meeting.


But it DOES earn you the right to a few more lines, to prove relevance, & find an unknown for them, that’s truly valuable.  


 6️⃣   I’d avoid the words in the list below…. Because they are the top-hits, of words that can cause an email deliverability problem.

  
 


OK that’s it!
See y’all tomorrow—  Or as some would say, remain. 🙂

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
…..5 changes that I made to my cold emails, that resulted in booking 35 SQLs consistently a month, on outbound. 🙂


1️⃣   Fewer mentions of your company name, the better. If you HAVE to do it, I’d do it in the 2nd to last line of the email.
2️⃣   No same subject lines. 
3️⃣   No more than 50 emails, per day. 
4️⃣   Ask a question— without adjectives or adverbs, that’s anchored to their buyer persona metrics. 

EX. I wouldn’t use terms like—-> High, low, fast, slow, good, bad, ineffective. 

A buyer can’t answer a question with adjectives, & it communicates you’re not after finding an unknown problem. 



If I say:

“do you have bad cold emails”,
“do you have low open rates”, or
“is your lead routing process ineffective?”….

They can’t really answer that question.



What is a bad email? 
What is a low open rate? 
What is ineffective? 

Best case scenario, they’ll answer yo the best of their ability….

But that gives you THEIR definition of low.



So you can’t use it to diagnose. 


FOR EXAMPLE:

If I know that <50% open rate, means they have a email deliverability problem…

And I ask “do you have a low open rate?”

And they say no….
(or yes)

I still don’t know don’t know their open rate,
so I still don’t know if they have the problem. 



Generally those questions are used to get the buyer to “admit” they have a problem—

& they know it’s a trap, so they shut down.



But the WORST part, is if you’re asking “are you struggling with low open rates?”

If they can say yes, it’s because they already know it. 



But if their open rate was bad enough, based on what they know, to do something about….

They would already have done something about it.



But you’re going outbound. 



Which means based on what they know, it’s not bad enough to warrant action. 

Or it’s a fringe problem at best.



But to cause a new initiative…

I have to find a new problem that they weren’t aware of before—

Which I can’t do, or can’t find, by asking a question with adjectives, or adverbs. 




So instead of asking “do you have low open rates?” I can ask:

▪️  is your open rate below 50%? OR
▪️  what is your open rate?

Reason I ask is industry average is 15% - 25%, & anything below 50% means that there’s an email deliverability problem. 

Only 59% of cold emails on average are delivered to the primary inbox—

Which means 41% of emails aren’t even reaching the prospect, & is typically a bigger driver to not hitting SQL quota….

Over email quality. 





5️⃣  use TRUE personalization, in the first line of the cold email. 


Not name, title, or company vertical….

But something that could only have been written to them. 




I put the personalization buckets below. 

They’re ranked in order of response rate— 

Left to right, for the buckets. 
& top to bottom, for the list underneath. 






OK that’s it. 🙂
Peace be to journey, y’all.


& good luck to all my cold emailers out there— It’s one of the hardest jobs (IMO), that you can do. 

So I promise, it gets easier. :)

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
In sales, if you’re struggling to know (or find) what “problem you solve” for your buyer….



The fastest way to understand a buyer, is to understand their number.

& the fastest way to understand the problems they want to solve, 


is to find the things, that affect that number.





Your buyers don’t solve problems in a vacuum.



They’re trying to solve problems, in relation to the main metric that defines whether they’re winning or losing, in their role.




This is Also Known As their lagging indicator metric, and it’s their final number.

The one they’re measured against.

And the one they’re ultimately hired, fired, promoted, or demoted to drive.





And THAT’s how they define a “problem”,

Is something that they need to change, that is currently affecting…..

Or will affect that lagging metric in the future enough, to make it worth solving now.





That’s how they determine what  to prioritize.



That’s how they define pain….

Is the volume they’re lacking, according to that metric.


That’s how they define impact…. 

Is what’s happening because of the delta according to that metric. 





In short

The driving business problem that they’re looking to solve for, ALWAYS ties back to the metric that person is accountable to….

Day in, & day out.




And if TRUE discovery happens, strong discovery happens, or true value is added—
That’s where it all starts.🙂

And that’s where it’s waiting to be found.

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
“Hyper-personalization”….. doesn’t exist. 


Personalization is 1:1. 
EX. I saw when you said “__”

Relevance is 1:many. 
…. Like a “cohort”. 

EX. Content downloads. Or CTOs. 



If you can send the same email to 2 peeps...

Then it's relevance-based.
Not personalized.



There’s 4 relevance-types.

▪️ Firmographic—relative to business traits. (EX. Segment, size)
▪️ Demographic—relative to human traits. (EX. Role.)
▪️ Technographic—relative to techstack. (EX. Outreach users)

& last but not least, trigger- based relevance. 


There’s 3 main triggers:
▪️ Inbound: Hand-raisers.
(demo-requests)

▪️ Postbound: non hand-raising, marketing actions.
(content downloads)

▪️ Bridgebound— Someone where an action occured, NOT re:your marketing.
(buyers who changed orgs)




In my view, in cold emails: “demographic relevance” (their role), is table stakes. 

Meaning it's centered on THEIR metrics. & finding unknowns, in regards to those metrics.

EX. a CFO= gross profit margin. & solves problems regarding it.




when you start to get good...
is when you add in techno/firmographic.

get great??
+ trigger-based relevance. 


But when you get REAL results, is when you use personalization. 





In 2026, personalization isn’t a luxury. Or a unicorn that you “should do, if you can”. 

It’s a must. 


In part, because it shows respect.
In part, because it shows research. & investment.
In part, because it builds credibility. 


But mostly, because it interrupts the pattern. 



The pattern of 99% of emails, is 1:many... Not 1:1.

Even with AI.
AI can’t:

— Pick the best personalization.
— Or meaningful personalization.
— Pick in-depth personalization.
— “Hook” it, to relevance.


For the "how":
If you want to combo the 2 most basic ingredients (personalization + role relevance), the formula is...

1st line = personalization. (w/ a title + a specific line)

2nd= 5 word hook + a question that (potentially) diagnoses a problem, that they likely have, & likely don’t know about.

NOT a “do you struggle with low open rates, & long sales cycles?”

If they already know about it...
But haven't moved yet: It's because it's not worth it. (based on what they know) 





To pump up the volume w/ a 3rd (+ trigger based)...

1st line= trigger + “but more importantly” + personalization.
EX. I saw you downloaded __, but more importantly, saw your article on __. 

2nd line, is same above. 




More below.
The 1-sheeter w/ 139 triggers, at the end.
+ 6 personalization buckets.



Bon courage a tous, out there.🙂


Don’t believe the dross that “personalization isn’t scalable”..
Or “it’s only for enterprise accounts”. 


Personalization, is for reps who want results.




This isn’t about showing them that we know them.

It's about showing them that we know something ABOUT them..
that they don’t already know.

& finding a goblin, they're unaware of. 👹



Off the soap box for me.
I’ll stop ranting now, promise. 🙂

But THAT’s sales.
That’s the type of rep, that we should & can be. :)

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Meant to say this last week, but got sick— I’m still REELING from The Sales Decade Project.


Where essentially 50 of the most elite female sellers, who sold in the 80s, all got together in one room.

So we could honor them, and meet them—
But most importantly, so we could learn from them. 

Learn how they did what they did, & how they were able to make such big strides.


And that room….
That day, was just electric.


But for days, I couldn’t figure out why.



Why it didn’t feel like a normal conference, or get together. 
Why the air felt so different. 


So I started to look for a through line.




I knew about 10 honorees beforehand— And had the chance to interview about 20 of them. 


And even though each person & story was different, 


They were all…

▪️ incredibly sharp. well learned.
▪️accountable for their outcomes, not effort. 
▪️ deeply technical. knew their buyer.
▪️& somehow, after all this time, they still had a deep love for sales.





The reason it was different, wasn’t because it was year 1.


It wasn’t because the banners had flashy colors, or because of cool swag giveaways. 


It was different… because they all had a love for sales, & not self. 

And had the same goal—>
Make the field & craft of sales better tomorrow, than it is today. 






Learning from the past, isn’t just about learning from mistakes. It’s also about learning what’s already been done.

So you don’t solve the same problem twice.



& if I took away nothing else from these women— It was that learning, true learning,

is a team sport. 


and real progress is made, only as a group.



Even with ALL of their experience, when someone was presenting, the other women, weren’t in the back of the room.


They weren’t on calls.
Or checking email.

They were in the front row…
Taking notes, full attention on the speaker.


After decades of experience…
They didn’t have the mindset that there was nothing left to learn. 

They were front & center, pen & paper out, taking notes…
Even after all this time.


They weren’t worried about if anyone “stole their ideas”. 
if anything, they were worried if they DIDN’T.





They all had the mindset, that great ideas are only helpful, when they’re shared.


And they don’t travel very far, or have much impact..
If they’re only known by one.


& that’s when it hit me.
That was the difference.


That’s not only what they knew— But what they embodied.


They were rooting for one another, & learning from one another…

And always thinking global, not local.



ANY RATE, just wanted to say thank you to all of the honorees— for their time, & sharing their stories with me.


And of course a massive, evergreen thank you to Lori Richardson—

For including me:
In your event, in your life, & for being my mentor, truly, since day 1. 




And Dr. Howard Dover for hosting at the UTD campus.




That’s it. 
My heart’s officially full. 

pic from “after hours”, post event— Because Lori is a 24/7 powerhouse. 



it’s all about:
We, not me, y’all. 🙂

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 month ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
…Ryan Zink, CFE is one of the most elite CEOs in the game, for one fundamental reason: He knows the buyer.


He is the buyer.
He was the buyer.


He knows what it’s like to start a franchise.
& intimately knows the day to day. 


What to watch out for—
What to consider in years 1, 2, and 10. 

What it takes to be successful.
What can lead to …well…. Not. 



Simply put—

His knowledge about franchising, grips further, than any polite or smooth talking conversation ever could.





He’s a good leader.
He pays attention to the small things.


He’s detailed. 
And has your back.

He’s a nice guy.
Fun & easy to talk to—




But that’s not why people trust him.



They trust him because of what he knows.

his level of expertise.
depth of experience.

And the breadth of knowledge,
that he’s built and accrued over the years. 






They don’t trust him because he’s nice.
Or fun to hang with—


His customers, team, & prospective buyers trust him….

Because they should.




Because they know his opinions, are pulling from decades of experience.


And when he gives advice on what you should do next, it’s just…..

Correct. 





Short clip of a convo I got to have with him live.

And full episode in the comments below. 




But the punchline is—
There is no amount of sales techniques, openers, phrases, reverses, or tricks….

That will ever outrun buyer knowledge. 




How you REALLY show that you care about your buyer, is learning about them.


Learning what they know.
Learning what they don’t. 

Industry averages.
Statistics on what’s normal.
Statistics on what’s a red flag.




So when you give your advice, on a call, of what they should do, buy, deploy, prioritize, or fall by the wayside….


It’s correct. 
& they can trust you,
because they SHOULD trust you. 




That’s more important than any objection handling technique, will ever be.



it’s more important to the buyer…
So it should be equally important to us.

author.name: Becc Holland
author.info: CEO @ Flip the Script

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
It’s Friday 3:41p in Texas….One QUICK to do before you log off for the weekend. 🙂



Take one buyer persona—
(**Example below for a COO)


Pull 30 stats that they’re measured on….

The industry average of the KPIs
& at what point they cause a problem. 

& spend 15 minutes studying them. 


That’s it. 




Remember, the things that are easy to do…
Are also easy NOT to do.


Bon weekend, y’all. 🙂

author.name: Becc Holland

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Here’s 5 Cold Email tips, that I live by..….


▪️ Fewer links, the better. 1 MAX.
▪️ 4 - 5 lines TOPS.
▪️ Don’t combo two sentences, into one paragraph.

▪️ There has to be at least one piece of (unique) value, to each email. Such as…..

    ——> asking a question about something,  that they didn’t likely know. 
    ——> dropping a statistic, that they likely didn’t know. 
    ——> including a pc. of 3rd party content.


▪️ And I’d try to avoid the death-trap firey, volcano words below, at all cost. 



OK, that’s it. 🙂
toodles, y’all.

author.name: Thibaut Souyris
author.info: The Sales Creator Agent | I help Sales Creators find, negotiate, and close brand partnerships

postedAt.postedAgoText: 10 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I met 20+ Creators in person in the past 2 weeks.

They all said a variation of the same thing:

"We want to spend more time with other creators, in person."

I spend most of my time working from home, in San Miguel de Allende, Mexico.

And the opportunities to meet my creators in person are incredibly rare.

In July 2025, I organized a Sales Creator Content Party, and it was the starting point of The Sales Creator Collective.

And after a bit less than a year, I feel like we don't have enough opportunities for our community to meet, exchange, and learn.

That's why I'm working on the first Creator Summit in Mexico City!

The concept is simple: 2 days in person event in CDMX for around 100 people.

Here's what I'm thinking of:

Day 1: Brand Content

The first day will be dedicated to creating content for brands with creators we have matched for them.

We'll create long-form assets, with usage rights and distribution on the creators channels.

Evening 1: Invite-only creator dinner

After a busy day of recording, we’ll head to a private location to relax and discover what Mexican hospitality is all about.

I’m thinking of an intimate setting with around 30 people max.

The goal is to create a moment of intimate conversations so creators and selected brands can spend some time building stronger relationships.

Day 2: Masterclasses

Day 2 will be all about sharing knowledge in a structured setup.

I’m thinking of inviting creators who are incredibly successful in their niche so they can share their experience in a structured masterclass format or a fireside chat setup.

The goal is to help established creators share their knowledge with Emerging Creators who will have purchased a ticket to join the event.

Evening 2: Mexico City Night Out

After the masterclass event is over, we’ll throw a networking mixer and take the crowd for a crazy night out in Mexico City.

I’m thinking sunset rooftop, speakeasy jazz/latino club, and after hours tacos.

We’ll give recommendations for places to go for recovering the morning after 😂

What I need from my audience:
- if you're a brand and want to sponsor this event, send me a DM
- if you're a creator and you want to join, tell me in the comments
- if you're not a creator but you'd like to purchase a ticket, tag someone you'd like to come with and I'll share an early bird code for the both of you.

In terms of dates, I'm thinking late October, early November.

Who's down?

author.name: Thibaut Souyris
author.info: The Sales Creator Agent | I help Sales Creators find, negotiate, and close brand partnerships

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I spent last Thursday in Barcelona with 4 sales creators.

They are all saying the same thing:

It takes around 10 days for their posts to reach their full impression potential.

For years, LinkedIn posts were all about early traction.

If you could get a ton of comments and reactions in the first 30 - 60 minutes after posting, you'd be guaranteed to have a viral post.

In June 2026, this tactic doesn't work anymore.

No matter how much engagement I get early on, I barely get over 2,000 impressions in the first 24 hours after I post.

But if I go back 10 days later, some of my posts get 5X the impressions I got in the first 24 hours.

And it's something I've seen for almost all the creators I represent.

My assumption?

LinkedIn is actively fighting engagement pods and completely killing early virality.

And I think it's a great thing.

Your content gets distributed over 10 days, and you go from a 24 hours window of opportunity to a 10 days window.

What do you think? Any other explanation?

postedAt.postedAgoText: 4 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Barcelona in the books!

Yesterday I drove from South of France to Barcelona to meet people I knew from LinkedIn but never met in person (except Alan Ruchtein)!

We had lunch and spent the afternoon walking around Barcelona and brainstorming with Andrei Zinkevich!

Then we met with Alan Ruchtein, Christian Krause, and Kevin Meyer for a fantastic Argentinian dinner.

This kind of moment really makes my job incredibly rewarding.

I spend most of my year working from Mexico behind my keyboard, but the opportunities to meet with fellow creators are rare.

And at the end of this day, I came to a simple conclusion: hanging out in person is what people want.

We're more connected than ever, but we tend to be physically isolated from the creator community.

Yesterday was a wake up call for me.

We need to create spaces and traditions that bring us together!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I met so many great people I would have never met if it wasn't for LinkedIn.

In December 2019, I decided to start posting content on LinkedIn.

Every weekday since that date, I shared a post (almost 7 years of daily posting).

This exercise helped me structure my thoughts, build in public, and become a Top Voice in the process.

But the most important thing it did for me was to create a massive brand recognition among my peers.

When I go to an event, I get stopped all the time by people who have been following my adventures for years.

Michael Rap is one of them.

I had never had a conversation with him in person before last week, but I got to hang out with him at Revenue Royale and I felt like meeting an old friend.

Most of my professional life is spent online, but meeting great people in person is the real reward for all that work.

Michael, loved meeting you!

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I'm on my way to making $1.2M in revenues in 2026.

Last year, I made less than $80,000.

My goal is to build a $10M business in 10 years (25% growth every year).

I know for a fact that I won't get to $10M by doing what I'm doing right now.

And one of the growth tactics I'm exploring is to build a completely different offering for brands we're working with.

Right now we run simple campaigns. 1 LinkedIn post per month for 3 to 6 months and an additional platform if it makes sense.

Brings good money, easy to sell, but very limited in terms of impact.

So last week, we had a chat with one of my MVPs, Edvin Vosylius, and he gave me a really good idea.

Why don't we build a complete offering for brands that includes the best creators of my rosters, and get them to collaborate on something unique.

Some ideas include:

- a content retreat with 5 to 10 big names to shoot a ton of content and collabs
- push this content to the creators' audiences
- get usage rights for brands to create whitelisted ads

I have a million ideas, but I need some people to explore these ideas with me.

So if you're a brand and you're looking for a way to stand out when working with influencers, I want to chat with you.

Drop me a DM or tag someone you think should see that.

author.name: Thibaut Souyris
author.info: The Sales Creator Agent | I help Sales Creators find, negotiate, and close brand partnerships

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I've been running campaigns with 60+ brands since September 2025.

Some of them were great, some of them were terrible, but most of them were in between.

Not great, not bad. But a lot of missed opportunities to convert real influence from the content created by our creators into real money.

Most brands start thinking about working with creators when they do a launch.

They scramble to source creators, ask them for a one-off post, pay $30,000+ for the campaign and get limited results.

With this budget size, it doesn't work.

Instead, my honest recommendation is to spend this $30,000 over 3 months with 5 to 10 creators.

You get them to post once a month to amplify your launch, and you evaluate who are the ones who qualify as brand ambassadors so you can renew them.

This reduces your risk and creators would rather accept a bit less money for a longer commitment than just a one off.

I did a whole presentation on that topic last week at Revenue Royale.

Go check it here and tag someone who needs to see this.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

document.title: Why Your LinkedIn Creator Campaign Failed

content:
I had a discovery call I thought went great.

The prospect was friendly.
Lots of nodding.
Lots of “yeah, exactly.”

Every time they said something, I agreed.
When they raised a concern, I softened.
When they hesitated, I rushed in to smooth it over.

The call felt pleasant.

I hung up feeling good.

They never bought.

A few weeks later, I listened back to the recording.

That’s when I heard it.

I didn’t challenge a single thing they said.
I didn’t offer a point of view.

I was focused on being likable.
I was auditioning.
I was adjusting what I said based on what I thought they wanted to hear.
I avoided pushing back.
I edited my opinions.
I prioritized harmony over honesty.

That was the problem.

When prospects sense you’re telling them what you think they want to hear rather than what they need to hear, they don’t trust you.

Later, a mentor said something that stuck:

“Buyers don’t need you to agree with them. They need you to help them think.”

That was the shift.

Respect didn’t come from being nice.
It came from:

Naming trade-offs.
Challenging assumptions.
Asking uncomfortable questions.
Being willing to say,
“That might not be the root cause of the issue.”

The irony?

The less I tried to be liked,
the more sales I made because people trusted me.

And in a world of similar products and services, trust is why people choose you.

No trust.
No transaction.

author.name: Josh Braun

postedAt.postedAgoText: 7 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I bought a GE refrigerator with a warranty.

Then one day the drawers decided they no longer believed in opening or closing.

“No problem,” I thought.
“That’s what warranties are for.”

Naive.
Apparently a warranty isn’t a repair program.
It’s an escape room.

Call this number.
Now call this number.
Now wait for parts.
The parts arrived.
Wrong parts.
Schedule another technician.
Earliest appointment: sometime before the next ice age.

Companies obsess over making the sale.

Then accidentally turn their support process into a endurance test.

The product breaks once.

The customer experience breaks over and over.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 7 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I told someone I lost my mother.

They immediately told me about losing theirs.

Them: “Yeah, when my mom died, I remember…”

It came from a good place.

But in that moment, the spotlight moved.
Off my mom.
Onto theirs.

When that happens, the other person never gets to share what they came to share.

Most people don’t do it on purpose.
That’s what makes it so easy to miss.

Keeping the spotlight on someone else is a skill.

It takes self-awareness.

Here’s a better response:
“Oh gosh. I’m so sorry. What’s your fondest memory of your mom?”

Now the spotlight stays where it belongs.
On them.

This week: 
Begin to notice where the spotlight is.
Notice how you make other people feel when you’re curious about what they said.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 9 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Why people don’t buy. 

A homeowner notices a small crack in the wall.

A handyman knocks on the door.

“Want me to fix it?”

The homeowner says no.

Why?

Not because they don’t want a better house.

Maybe they’re leaving for vacation next week. Bad timing.

Maybe they don’t trust the handyman.

Maybe they can’t decide which handyman to use. 

Or maybe they’re staring at a leaking roof, a broken AC, and a kitchen remodel. Compared to those, the crack just isn’t a big enough problem.

That’s how buying decisions work.

When people don’t buy, it’s because:

The timing isn’t right.
They don’t trust you.
They can’t decide on the solution.
Your problem loses to a bigger one.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
In selling, we have a strong holding-on muscle.

We chase. 
We follow up. 
We circle back.

But we never train the letting-go muscle.

Here’s how I strengthen mine.

When a prospect no-shows a meeting, I do nothing.

About 70% of the time, they catch it themselves. 
They apologize. 
They reschedule.

The other 30% stay quiet.
I let them.
I’m not chasing. 
I’m testing for motivation.
I add them to my nurture track and move on. 

I don’t play catch with people who keep the ball.

How strong is your letting-go muscle?

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I’m about to walk on stage.

I’ve been doing this for over 10 years.
And every single time…
…I get nervous.

I used to think the goal was to get rid of the nerves.

It isn’t.
Now I just say, “Hi.”
Thanks for showing up.
Thanks for reminding me that I care.

The nerves aren’t the bad part of me.
They’re one of the good parts.

I don’t wait until I feel fearless.
I walk on stage nervous.

For the first four minutes, my heart races. 

My mouth is a little dry. 
My brain wonders if I’ve forgotten how to speak.

Then something shifts.
I stop thinking about myself.
I start thinking about the people in the room.

And that’s when the nerves take a back seat.

Confidence isn’t the absence of nerves.
It’s learning to make room for them.

Do it nervous.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 6 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I had a good friend in college named Seth.

He drank until he passed out. 
Most nights.

So I did what I thought a good friend does.
I lectured him.

I told him about his liver. 
I pitched him on rehab. 

I gave him the full TED talk on why he had to change.

He didn’t change.
He just pulled away. 
And eventually I lost my friend. 

Here’s what I got wrong.

I stood top of a tree, like a wise monkey shouting at a fish.

“Hey Seth Fish. Get out of that pond. Come live up here in my tree.”

Fish don’t do well in trees.

What I wish I’d done was simple.
Walk around and sat next to him in the pond. 
Slide
 him a coffee.
“Morning, man. Rough one?”

No judgment. 
No fixing. 
Just next to him.

I can’t do that with Seth anymore.
He passed away.

But I carry it now.
Because the moment I try to “help” someone, I’m telling them I’m one up. That I know better.

Sitting beside someone and supporting them, with no judgment, is a different thing entirely.

That skill made me a better salesperson.

But more than that, it made me a better person.

We live in a world that’s loud and divisive, where everyone is starving to feel heard.

Here’s the one thing I know to be true.

People don’t resist change.
They resist being changed.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I’m giving a talk at an SKO.

Mid-sentence, I notice a few people tapping away on their phones.

And my brain does what brains do.

“They’re bored.”
“I’m losing them.”
“This is officially bombing.”

Cue the internal Netflix documentary:
The Rise and Fall of Josh Braun.

Then I caught it. And asked myself “What else might be happening?”

What if they’re taking notes?
What if they’re Slacking their team: “This part’s good”?
What if someone’s dealing with a small fire at home that has nothing to do with me?

Nothing in the room actually changed.
Only the story I told myself about what I was seeing.

Same phones.
Completely different meaning.

And almost instantly, my body followed the new story.

Shoulders dropped.
Voice steadied.
Energy came back.

Here’s the thing;

The mind is like a bad sports commentator.
It sees one play and immediately declares the season over.

No replay.
No context.

Our feelings don’t come from what happens.

They come from the meaning we attach to what happens.

Change the story,
and you change how you feel.

The skill isn’t positive thinking.
It’s story awareness.

Catching yourself mid-narration and remembering:
“This is just one possible script.”

Most anxiety isn’t caused by the situation.
It’s caused by the story we sprint ahead and tell ourselves about the situation.

Notice the story.
Don’t believe the first draft.
Rewrite if needed.

That’s the practice.
Simple, not easy.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
If it sounds like there’s a fit, don’t make the prospect figure out what happens next.

Sales is about leadership.
Not control.
Not persuasion.
Leadership.

If someone shares a problem you can help solve, your job is to gently and elegantly recommending a next step.

Here’s what I say: 

“Thanks for sharing about the event. Does it make sense fo you to see slides of what’s covered in the workshop sometime in the next week or two or would you rather leave it here?”

Notice what’s happening here:
It’s focused on what they get.
It’s specific.
It’s easy to say yes to.

It’s easy to say no to.
That last part matters.

Because I’m not trying to manufacture momentum.
I’m testing motivation.

People who want to solve a problem move toward it.

People who don’t, won’t.
That’s why “no” is a perfectly good answer.
A no creates clarity.

A maybe delays it.
The goal of a next step isn’t to push the deal forward.
It’s to find out whether the buyer wants to.

“Based on what you’ve shared, the next logical step might be X. Would you be open to that, or would you rather leave it here?”

They keep their autonomy.
They have complete freedom to say no.

And their answer tells you everything you need to know.

The goal isn’t to get a yes.
It’s to find out if people are motivated to buy. 

You don’t create motivation, you align with it. 

You can’t play catch with people who keep the ball.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Psychologist Daniel Kahneman described two modes of thinking.

System 1: fast, intuitive, automatic. What’s 3x3

System 2: slow, deliberate, effortful. What’s 17x23?

When you interrupt someone with a cold call, they’re not sitting around waiting to analyze a complex question.

They’re answering email.
Reviewing a forecast.
Trying to finish a report before lunch.

So the more mental effort your question requires, the more likely they are to shut down.

Compare these two.

System 1:
“How do you currently track down receipts and purchase details when purchases are happening all over the business? Is there manual detective work involved, or does it happen automatically?”

Most people can answer that immediately.

Now this.
System 2:
“How much time does your finance team spend each month manually reconciling transactions, collecting receipts, and correcting coding errors, and what impact does that have on the efficiency of your month-end close?”

To answer that, they have to stop.
Estimate.
Calculate.
Analyze.

That’s work.
And people avoid work.
Especially on cold calls.

The goal isn’t to ask shallow questions.
It’s to ask questions that are easy to answer and make people think differently. 

Reduce the cognitive load.
You’ll have more conversations.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Treat sales like a video game.

Why?

A game is just a fun, rule-based activity. 
That’s it.

You stop taking it so seriously.

Setbacks aren’t the end of the world.
They’re obstacles.
Part of playing.

You expect obstacles when you play.
You don’t freak out when they show up.

Same in sales.
A game feels like a challenge, not a threat.
You level up.
You don’t give up.

You learn nos are part of playing.
One path doesn’t work?
Take another.

Respawn.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
My yoga teacher said something that stuck.

I told her warrior 3 was hard.

She said,
“Is it hard or unfamiliar?”

Hard sounds permanent.
Like a limit.
Like something you might not be cut out for.

Unfamiliar is temporary.
It just means your brain hasn’t built the pathway yet.

We quit a lot of things too early because we mistake unfamiliar for hard.

If you relabel the experience, you change your relationship to it.

“This is hard” makes you brace.
“This is unfamiliar” invites practice.

Same task.
Different experience.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I agreed to a meeting with a salesperson.

Then they confirmed it.

Then they confirmed it again.

Then, an hour before, they confirmed it a third time.

Your boss doesn’t confirm a meeting.

Your coworkers don’t confirm a meeting.

The people who belong in your calendar assume they belong there.

They booked the time. 
You said yes. 
Done.

Confirming once, okay. 

Confirming three times isn’t diligence.
It’s a tell.
It says:
“I’m not sure you actually want this, so I keep checking.”

And the moment I feel you checking, I stop wanting it.

Colleagues don’t confirm meetings three times.

The nervous do.

author.name: Josh Braun
author.info: Struggling to book meetings? Getting ghosted? Want to sell without pushing, convincing, or begging? Read this profile.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I wanted to know how much my friend's watch cost.

But asking:

"How much was that watch?"

Feels invasive.
Too direct.
Coming in too hot.

So instead I said:

"I read the Panda Daytona is impossible to get. You probably paid $45,000 on the grey market."

He laughed.

"No. I got it from an authorized dealer for $17,000."

I had my answer.

Why?

Because people don't like answering questions.

They like correcting mistakes.

That's why:

"How much does your company spend on recruiting?"

Gets a vague answer.

But:

"You're probably spending at least $500k a year on recruiters."

Gets:

"No, nowhere near that. More like $200k."

Same information.
Different path.

The fastest way to the truth isn't asking for it.

It's getting it wrong.
